original_reasoning: Arabic: الشرف = elevation, high place, nobility, height — the camel's hump is high, the battlements are high. Greek: high-roofed, high-ceilinged. Both share the mafahim of upward elevation/height as a marker of status and physical structure. Clear shared concept.

Arabic الكركم = saffron (الزعفران) and turmeric — a golden-yellow spice/dye substance derived from the crocus/curcuma plant. Greek κροκόμαγμα = the residue/pressed-out remains of saffron unguent (from κρόκος = saffron). Both directly involve the saffron/crocus substance — Arabic as the raw spice, Greek as the saffron extract residue. Strong semantic connection via shared saffron-substance domain.
both express war/combat — strong semantic match
Arabic gives shrinking downward or becoming stunted toward the ground, and Greek means shrunken or withered; the conceptual chain is clear.
ἠνείχοντο suggests enduring or holding oneself up, which maps well onto خنطه as burden, distress, or pressure borne.
Beauty and excellence naturally lead into exultation or rejoicing, so the deeper conceptual chain is fairly strong.
Arabic denotes preventing by means of a restraining object in the mouth, while Greek means they have held; this yields a clear holding-restraint chain.
Arabic includes النجيل, a leafy plant/grass, and Greek gloss points to asphodel or daffodil; plant-reference continuity is plausible.
